text: The surgical procedure is conducted using four trocars with the patient positioned on their left side. The surgeon identifies the previous ileocolic anastomosis and performs a peritumoral injection of indocyanin green. Due to the absence of the heliocolic pedicle, a lateral-medial approach is used. The surgeon sections the lateral lesion and identifies the main landmarks for heliocolic resection, including the duodenum and the right ureter.

The surgeon verifies the medial limit of resection using fluorescence guidance 25 minutes after ICG injection. The superior mesenteric vein and some ileal branches are identified. The small intestine is isolated approximately fifteen centimeters from the previous ileocolic anastomosis. A mesocolic excision is performed, following the superior mesenteric vein in a caudal to cranial direction up to the pancreatic head, ensuring removal of all fluorescent lymphatic tissue.

The surgeon dissects the preserved middle colic vessels from the previous right colectomy. An ileocolic anastomosis is performed using a stapler, following verification of the vascularization of the intestinal stumps with an intraoperative intravenous injection of indocyanin green (ICG). Hemostasis of the intraluminal side of the anastomosis is confirmed, and the service bridge is sutured using a V-lock suture.
